filter_index.video_title: Introducing The Ranthan Kharak Trek | Our First Trek In Kumaon Himalayas | Indiahikes

snow_or_rain_notes: Snow can start during the early weeks of November
Late weeks of April: snow towards Ranthan Kharak
May: snow starts to recede at higher altitudes
Spring: patches of snow as you ascend higher on the trek
End of October/start of November: chances of witnessing the first winter snowfall of the year if weather turns bad
temperature_notes: Autumn: 12°C to 18°C (day); night can drop to 0°C; 4-5 warm layers required
Summer: 15°C to 20°C (day); night drops to almost 2°C; 3 warm layers required
Spring: 10°C to 15°C (day); night can hit sub-zero; 4-5 warm layers required; chilling winds after crossing Bajimanian Kharak

terrain_challenges: Tricky climb to Thal Tok on Day 4
Steep ascent through rugged, boulder-strewn terrain on Day 5
Final climb to Ranthan Top is the most demanding part of the trek
Day 5 summit climb is steep and demanding
altitude_or_weather_risks: From Namik to Baji Kharak, ascend over 2,300 feet; stay well-hydrated to lower risk of Acute Mountain Sickness
Ranthan Top is at 12,887 ft; above 10,000 ft AMS risk is real
Altitude sickness can affect first-time and experienced trekkers alike; even fit persons climbing too high too soon are at equal risk
During Spring, pack 4-5 warm layers; layering helps against chilling winds, especially after crossing Bajimanian Kharak
Rainfall or snowfall lasting hours can change trek dynamics; rain can flood trails or make them slushy and unsafe

fitness_benchmark: To do the Ranthan Kharak Trek comfortably, you must be able to walk, jog or run 5 km in 35 minutes. This is the minimum recommended fitness level that you must aim for.
approval_requirements: Make sure you upload these screenshots 15 to 20 days before your trek date. We want to see that you are being consistent in your preparation.
Your screenshots must demonstrate that you are meeting the fitness criteria for your trek.
Your runs don't meet your trek's fitness criteria at least 3 times in the past month.
prep_window: Follow this, and you'll be trek-ready in 6 weeks.
Make sure you upload these screenshots 15 to 20 days before your trek date.

experience_themes: Autumn: sharper, crisper mountain views from Ranthan Top; trail changes from greens near base camp to yellows, oranges, and browns in meadows; magnificent ridge walk views
Summer: shades of green throughout the trek; wildflowers blooming on meadows and campsites in June; bright green grasslands with bright yellow flowers; mountain views open up on clear days

Spring: lush green forests with rhododendrons, trees, mushrooms, birds and animals; hidden waterfalls and water trickling through thickets; budding green grass with wildflowers; possible Rhododendron bloom (reds and pinks) at end of spring/beginning of summer
Autumn: mountain views from Ranthan Top are stunning; sharper and crisper views than summer; magnificent views on both sides of the ridge walk
Summer: mountain views start opening up on clear days
Spring: views of far-off mountains will not be crystal clear, but proximity gives a magnified view
primary_watchouts: Early Spring: may not be able to go all the way to Ranthan Kharak or may have to tackle/navigate sections of snow
End of October/start of November: weather can turn bad with chances of first winter snowfall
After crossing Bajimanian Kharak in Spring: chilling winds on exposed parts
Autumn season is shorter compared to summer
Trekkers wanting to go all the way to Ranthan Kharak in early Spring (snow may block or limit access)
Trekkers seeking crystal-clear far-off mountain views in Spring
best_for: Autumn trekkers seeking sharp, crisp mountain views from Ranthan Top and ridge walk vistas
Summer trekkers wanting wildflowers, lush green meadows, and clear-day mountain views
Spring trekkers seeking Rhododendron blooms, lush green forests, hidden waterfalls, and biodiversity
not_ideal_for: Trekkers wanting to go all the way to Ranthan Kharak in early Spring (snow may block or limit access)
Trekkers seeking crystal-clear far-off mountain views in Spring
